description: Figure 11 presents comparative morphologies of impact fracture surfaces for low-nitrogen (LN, 0.0025 wt% N) and high-nitrogen (HN, 0.1326 wt% N) Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steels. Each row comprises three panels: left shows 3D topographic maps (superdepth microscopy) revealing surface height distributions with maximum heights of 7.53 mm for LN and 9.95 mm for HN steels; center displays low-magnification SEM images (1 mm scale bar) illustrating overall fracture surfaces comprising fibrous, radiation, and shear lip zones; right presents high-magnification SEM images (10 μm scale bar) showing micro-scale dimple characteristics. The LN steel exhibits elongated dimples aligned with crack propagation direction, indicating accelerated crack growth under shear stress, while the HN steel displays larger, uniform equiaxed dimples with higher average depth, signifying enhanced plastic deformation and energy absorption during impact testing. Aggregated AlN inclusions are visible within dimples of the HN steel. The HN sample shows a significantly higher shear lip zone area ratio compared to LN, correlating with its superior impact toughness (130.5 J versus 81 J) and slower crack propagation rate attributed to grain refinement and reduced ferrite content.
